answer:
In Allama Iqbal's poem "Kinaar-e-Ravi" ("At the Bank of the Ravi") from *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, the speaker stands at the Ravi riverbank during a silent evening, entering a contemplative and prayerful state amid the river's song-like flow.[^1] He reflects on the sacred transformation of the world in this moment, a disorienting sense of place amid twilight's beauty (likened to wine-stained skies and sown sunflowers), the distant minarets and ruined palace evoking time's tyranny and lost history, and—most centrally—a boat racing across the river as a metaphor for human life's ceaseless, eternal journey: appearing briefly in sight before vanishing into eternity, yet never perishing.[^2][^3]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 28 "Kinaar-e-Ravi" (translation), [para. p-70a0dc](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/28-kinaar-e-ravi/translation/#p-70a0dc).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 28 "Kinaar-e-Ravi" (translation), [paras. p-7ee613 to p-6b8789](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/28-kinaar-e-ravi/translation/#p-7ee613-p-6b8789).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 28 "Kinaar-e-Ravi" (translation), [paras. p-8c38f2 to p-1c94bb](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/28-kinaar-e-ravi/translation/#p-8c38f2-p-1c94bb).
